title: Rag rug coiled on a frame loom
subtitle: A circular woven rag rug made from old fabric strips on a frame loom using a coiled construction
excerpt: Weave a circular rag rug on a frame loom using long strips cut from old cotton or denim fabric. The circular form comes from starting at the centre and working outward in a coiled weave. The finished rug is about 40 cm across and is thick enough to use as a bath mat or bedroom rug.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from traditional rag-rug construction practice. Circular coiled rugs on frame looms are documented in British and American craft traditions as a recycling and domestic textile project.

glossaryTerms.definition: A long narrow strip of fabric cut from recycled clothing or household textiles and used as the weft in a rag rug. Strips are usually 1.5 to 2.5 cm wide for a dense, warm rug.
The structural threads tensioned on the loom, held under tension while the weft (rag strips) is woven through. For a circular rag rug, the warp radiates outward from the centre like spokes.
A weave construction in which the weft spirals outward from the centre of the frame loom rather than travelling in straight horizontal rows.

content.text: rag rug starts from the centre of the frame loom and builds outward using
. The technique is more forgiving than rectangular rag rug weaving because the spiral structure naturally adjusts as you add more material.

items.name: Old cotton shirts, sheets, or denim, cut into 2 cm wide strips
Strong cotton warp yarn or twine for the radial spokes
Square frame loom, minimum 45 cm by 45 cm

content.text: Step 1: Set up the radial warp'

Find the centre of the frame loom and mark it. Run warp threads from the centre to each of the four sides (the cardinal points first: top, bottom, left, right), then add four more diagonal spokes (top-left, top-right, bottom-left, bottom-right). You now have 8 spokes radiating from the centre. Tie all spokes to the frame at the outer edge and to a small central loop or ring at the centre.
Add a second ring of 8 spokes between the first 8, giving 16 spokes total. The more spokes you use, the more even the circular form; 16 is the minimum for a rug that does not look octagonal.
Step 3: Weave from the centre outward'

Begin weaving at the central ring, working a rag strip over and under each spoke in a spiral. After one full circle, move outward: at each spoke, go over the spoke you went under last time and under the one you went over. The over-under alternation locks the coil as you spiral outward. Weave outward until the rug fills the frame or reaches the desired diameter.

Cut the rug from the frame, leaving enough warp at each spoke to tie off in a secure overhand knot. Tuck the knot ends under the weave with a blunt needle. The finished rug can be backed with non-slip fabric for use on smooth floors.

items.problem: The rug is becoming hexagonal or octagonal rather than round
items.cause: Too few spokes to produce a smooth circular form
items.fix: Add more spokes (up to 24) before continuing. More spokes give a rounder form; fewer spokes produce a polygon shape.

items.problem: The rug is puckering and not lying flat
items.cause: The weft is being packed too tightly as the rug grows outward, pulling the spokes inward
items.fix: As the rug grows larger, the circumference increases and each coil of weft must be longer. Space the weft slightly looser as you work outward, allowing more weft per coil as the radius increases.
